question_text: Which of the following diseases is classified as a non-communicable disease because it cannot be transmitted from one person to another through any mode of contact or vector?
options: A. Influenza, which is caused by the influenza virus and spreads through respiratory droplets
B. Diabetes mellitus, which is caused by metabolic dysfunction involving insulin and cannot spread between individuals
C. Measles, which is caused by the measles virus and spreads through airborne droplets
D. Malaria, which is caused by the Plasmodium parasite and spreads through mosquito bites
explanation: Diabetes mellitus is a non-communicable disease (NCD) caused by metabolic dysfunction — either the pancreas does not produce enough insulin (Type 1) or the body cannot effectively use the insulin it produces (Type 2). It cannot be transmitted from one person to another. Influenza, measles and malaria are all communicable diseases.
elimination_hints: Influenza (A), measles (C) and malaria (D) are all caused by infectious agents that spread between people. Only diabetes (B) lacks an infectious cause and transmission route.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Which of the following correctly explains why Health Education emphasises prevention of disease over treatment, and how this approach benefits individuals and communities in the long term?
options: A. Prevention is emphasised because treatment is always free and unlimited in every country

B. Prevention is more cost-effective than treatment, reduces suffering and disability, maintains quality of life, decreases the burden on healthcare systems, and empowers individuals to take control of their own health through informed choices
C. Prevention is emphasised because treatment has been entirely abolished in modern healthcare
D. Prevention is less effective than treatment in all circumstances and is promoted only for economic reasons
explanation: Health Education emphasises prevention because: preventing disease is far more cost-effective than treating it; prevention avoids suffering, disability and loss of productivity; it maintains quality of life; reduces the burden on hospitals and healthcare systems; and empowers individuals to take proactive control of their health. The adage 'prevention is better than cure' captures this philosophy.
elimination_hints: A makes a false claim about free treatment; C is factually wrong; D contradicts the evidence base. B correctly articulates the multi-faceted case for prevention.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Analyze how poor sanitation and lack of access to clean drinking water in a community can lead to epidemic outbreaks of waterborne diseases, and identify which chain of events most accurately describes this process.
options: A. Poor sanitation has no connection to disease outbreaks and the two are completely unrelated phenomena

B. Poor sanitation allows human faeces containing pathogens to contaminate water sources; community members who consume this contaminated water ingest the pathogens; the pathogens multiply and cause disease; infected individuals continue the cycle by further contaminating the environment, leading to a rapid epidemic spread
C. Waterborne diseases spread only through air and have nothing to do with water contamination or sanitation
D. Clean water causes more disease than contaminated water because pathogens prefer clean environments
explanation: The chain: poor sanitation → faecal matter enters water sources → pathogens (cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A bacteria/viruses) contaminate drinking water → community members consume contaminated water → pathogens cause infection → infected individuals excrete more pathogens → further environmental contamination → rapid epidemic spread. Breaking this cycle through sanitation and clean water prevents outbreaks.
elimination_hints: A denies the well-established sanitation-disease link; C wrongly claims airborne spread; D makes an absurd claim. B correctly traces the faecal-oral contamination cycle.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Evaluate which of the following approaches to Health Education would be most effective in changing the health behaviours of a rural Indian community with low literacy rates, high prevalence of waterborne diseases, and limited access to television and internet.
options: A. Distributing complex English-language research papers on microbiology to every household in the village

B. Conducting interactive health education sessions using local language, visual aids, street plays (nukkad natak), demonstrations of handwashing and water purification, and training local community health workers as peer educators
C. Broadcasting health information only on international English-language satellite television channels
D. Sending text-message-only health alerts to smartphones, assuming every villager owns a smartphone with internet
explanation: For a rural community with low literacy, limited media access and specific waterborne disease problems: interactive sessions in the local language, visual aids (pictures, demonstrations), culturally resonant methods (street plays), practical demonstrations (handwashing, water purification) and trained local peer educators are most effective. This approach respects the community's language, literacy level, cultural context and available resources.
elimination_hints: A assumes high English literacy; C assumes satellite TV access; D assumes smartphone ownership. Only B adapts to the community's actual context — local language, visual/interactive methods, community health workers.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Which of the following correctly identifies the three main levels of disease prevention that form the framework of modern public health and Health Education strategies?
options: A. Surgical prevention, chemical prevention and nuclear prevention

B. Primary prevention (preventing disease before it occurs), secondary prevention (early detection and treatment to halt progression), and tertiary prevention (rehabilitation and minimising disability after disease has occurred)
C. First aid prevention, hospital prevention and pharmacy prevention
D. Morning prevention, afternoon prevention and evening prevention based on the time of day
explanation: The three levels: Primary prevention — preventing disease before it occurs (vaccination, health education, sanitation). Secondary prevention — early detection and prompt treatment to stop disease progression (screening, diagnosis). Tertiary prevention — rehabilitation, minimising disability and restoring function after disease has occurred (physiotherapy, support groups).
elimination_hints: A, C and D are not recognised public health frameworks. B correctly identifies the standard primary-secondary-tertiary prevention model used worldwide.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Which of the following vector-borne communicable diseases is transmitted to humans specifically through the bite of an infected female Aedes aegypti mosquito?
options: A. Tuberculosis, which spreads through airborne respiratory droplets
B. Dengue fever, which is caused by the dengue virus transmitted through Aedes mosquito bites
C. Cholera, which spreads through contaminated water and food
D. Ringworm, which spreads through direct skin contact with an infected person
explanation: Dengue fever is transmitted by the bite of an infected female Aedes aegypti mosquito carrying the dengue virus. The Aedes mosquito is also responsible for transmitting chikungunya and Zika virus. TB is airborne, cholera is waterborne, and ringworm is contact-transmitted.
elimination_hints: TB (A) is airborne; cholera (C) is waterborne; ringworm (D) is contact-based. Only dengue (B) involves Aedes mosquito transmission.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Analyze the relationship between rapid urbanisation in India and the increasing prevalence of non-communicable diseases such as obesity, diabetes and cardiovascular disease among the urban population.
options: A. Urbanisation has no connection to non-communicable diseases because these diseases are purely genetic

B. Rapid urbanisation leads to sedentary lifestyles due to desk-based jobs and motorised transport, increased consumption of processed and fast foods, higher stress levels, air pollution exposure and reduced physical activity — all of which are established risk factors for obesity, diabetes and cardiovascular disease
C. Urbanisation actually reduces the prevalence of all diseases because cities have more hospitals
D. Non-communicable diseases only affect rural populations and never occur in urban areas
explanation: Urbanisation drives NCD prevalence through multiple pathways: sedentary occupations (desk jobs), motorised transport replacing walking/cycling, availability and marketing of processed/fast foods, psychological stress from urban living, air pollution, and reduced opportunities for recreational physical activity. These lifestyle changes are established risk factors for obesity, diabetes and cardiovascular diseases.
elimination_hints: A ignores lifestyle factors; C confuses hospital availability with disease prevention; D contradicts epidemiological data showing higher urban NCD rates.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Which of the following is a recognised objective of Health Education that goes beyond simply providing information, and instead focuses on developing practical health-related skills among individuals?
options: A. Memorising the names and dates of all Nobel Prize winners in Medicine without understanding their discoveries
B. Developing practical skills such as proper handwashing technique, balanced meal preparation, basic first aid, reading food nutrition labels, and performing cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR)
C. Learning to diagnose complex diseases and perform surgical procedures without medical training
D. Developing skills in competitive sports like cricket and football, which are unrelated to health education
explanation: A key Health Education objective is developing practical health skills — not just knowledge. This includes proper handwashing, preparing balanced meals, reading nutrition labels, basic first aid, CPR, safe food handling, and personal hygiene practices. These are actionable skills that individuals can apply daily to protect and improve their health.
elimination_hints: A is rote memorisation without practical value; C exceeds Health Education's scope; D is about sports, not health skills. B correctly lists practical, actionable health skills.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Evaluate how the concept of 'herd immunity' relates to Health Education's emphasis on vaccination programmes, and explain why it is important even for individuals who cannot be vaccinated due to medical reasons.
options: A. Herd immunity is irrelevant because vaccination only protects the vaccinated individual and has no community-wide effect

B. When a sufficiently high proportion of a community is vaccinated, the spread of disease is significantly reduced because there are fewer susceptible hosts for the pathogen, indirectly protecting unvaccinated individuals such as infants, elderly and immunocompromised persons who cannot receive vaccines — this is herd immunity
C. Herd immunity means that once one person is vaccinated, the entire herd of cattle in the village becomes immune to all diseases
D. Herd immunity is achieved only when zero percent of the population is vaccinated
explanation: Herd immunity occurs when a large enough proportion of the population is immunised, reducing the number of susceptible hosts so significantly that the pathogen cannot easily find new people to infect. This indirectly protects vulnerable individuals who cannot be vaccinated (newborns, pregnant women, immunocompromised patients) because the chain of transmission is disrupted at the community level.
elimination_hints: A denies community-wide effects; C confuses with animal health; D states the opposite. B correctly explains herd immunity and its protective benefit for unvaccinated vulnerable individuals.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Analyze why Health Education programmes in schools are considered essential for creating a 'health-literate' generation that can make informed decisions about their own health throughout their lives.
options: A. School Health Education is irrelevant because children are naturally healthy and never need health information

B. School-based Health Education reaches children during their formative years when health attitudes and behaviours are being established, provides a foundation of health knowledge and critical thinking skills, and empowers students to make informed health decisions that persist into adulthood, creating lifelong healthy habits
C. School Health Education is important only for children who are already sick and serves no purpose for healthy students
D. Health Education in schools is needed only to pass examinations and has no real-world application beyond the classroom
explanation: Schools are the ideal setting for Health Education because children's health attitudes and behaviours are formed during formative years. Early education provides foundational health knowledge, develops critical thinking about health claims, and establishes healthy habits that persist into adulthood. This creates a health-literate generation capable of making informed decisions throughout their lives.
elimination_hints: A ignores childhood health needs; C limits to sick children only; D reduces it to exam preparation. B correctly captures the formative, lifelong impact of school-based Health Education.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Match the following types of disease-causing agents (pathogens) in Column A with the specific disease they cause in Column B.
column_b: P. Malaria — transmitted by Anopheles mosquito, causes fever and chills
Q. Cholera — causes severe watery diarrhoea and dehydration
R. Ringworm — causes circular itchy skin patches
S. Smallpox — caused blistering skin lesions (now eradicated globally)
explanation: Vibrio cholerae (bacteria) → cholera (Q). Variola major (virus) → smallpox (S). Trichophyton (fungus) → ringworm (R). Plasmodium falciparum (parasite) → malaria (P).
elimination_hints: Vibrio cholerae (1) obviously causes cholera (Q); Plasmodium (4) obviously causes malaria (P). Anchor with these two.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Match the following Health Education strategies in Column A with the specific health challenge they are most effectively designed to address in Column B.
column_a: 1. Mass vaccination campaign with community mobilisation and awareness
2. School-based nutrition education with practical cooking demonstrations
3. Anti-tobacco awareness campaign using graphic warning labels and media
4. Community sanitation drive with construction of toilets and handwashing stations
column_b: P. Preventing waterborne and faecal-oral diseases like cholera and typhoid through improved hygiene infrastructure
Q. Reducing childhood malnutrition and promoting balanced diet habits among school-age children
R. Preventing epidemic outbreaks of communicable diseases like polio and measles through herd immunity
S. Reducing tobacco consumption and preventing smoking-related cancers and cardiovascular diseases
explanation: Vaccination campaign → preventing epidemics through herd immunity (R). Nutrition education → reducing malnutrition, promoting balanced diet (Q). Anti-tobacco campaign → reducing smoking-related diseases (S). Sanitation drive → preventing waterborne diseases (P).
elimination_hints: Anti-tobacco campaign (3) clearly addresses smoking-related diseases (S); sanitation drive (4) clearly addresses waterborne diseases (P). Anchor with these.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Match the following prevention levels in Column A with the correct example of a health intervention at that level in Column B.
column_b: P. Regular blood pressure screening to detect hypertension early before it causes organ damage
Q. Cardiac rehabilitation programme for a patient recovering from a heart attack to restore function
R. Vaccination of children against measles to prevent the disease from ever occurring
explanation: Primary prevention → vaccination to prevent disease from occurring (R). Secondary prevention → screening for early detection before complications (P). Tertiary prevention → rehabilitation to restore function after disease (Q).
elimination_hints: Vaccination (R) prevents disease before it occurs = primary. Screening (P) detects existing disease early = secondary. Rehabilitation (Q) restores function after disease = tertiary.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below and select the correct option regarding the scope of Health Education.
assertion: Health Education covers not only physical health topics but also mental health awareness, nutrition education, substance abuse prevention, environmental health and reproductive health.
reason: The WHO definition of health encompasses physical, mental and social well-being, and therefore Health Education must address all these dimensions to be comprehensive and effective.
options: A. Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
B. Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
C. Assertion is true but Reason is false
D. Assertion is false but Reason is true
explanation: The Assertion is true — Health Education covers all listed topics. The Reason is also true and correctly explains why: since the WHO defines health as multi-dimensional (physical, mental, social), Health Education must address all dimensions to align with this comprehensive definition, which is exactly why its scope extends beyond physical health alone.
elimination_hints: Both statements are factually correct. The Reason provides the foundational rationale (WHO multi-dimensional definition) that explains the Assertion's broad scope.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below and select the correct option regarding the concept of epidemic and endemic diseases.
assertion: Malaria is considered endemic in many parts of India because it occurs at a consistently predictable rate in these regions year after year.
reason: An endemic disease is one that has been completely eradicated from a region and no longer occurs there.
options: A. Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
B. Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
C. Assertion is true but Reason is false
D. Assertion is false but Reason is true
explanation: The Assertion is true — malaria is endemic in many parts of India, occurring at a predictable, constant rate in these regions. However, the Reason is false — an endemic disease is one that is constantly present in a region at a predictable rate, NOT one that has been eradicated. The Reason describes the opposite of endemic; eradication means the disease has been eliminated.
elimination_hints: The Reason defines endemic as 'eradicated' — this is the exact opposite of the correct definition. Endemic means constantly present, not eliminated.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason below and select the correct option regarding the role of personal hygiene in preventing communicable diseases.
assertion: Practising personal hygiene measures such as regular handwashing with soap, bathing, oral care and wearing clean clothes significantly reduces the risk of contracting communicable diseases.
reason: Personal hygiene practices remove or reduce the number of pathogens on the body surface, prevent their entry into the body, and eliminate conditions that favour microbial growth, thereby breaking the chain of infection at the portal of entry level.
options: A. Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
B. Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
C. Assertion is true but Reason is false
D. Assertion is false but Reason is true
explanation: The Assertion is true — personal hygiene significantly reduces communicable disease risk. The Reason is also true and correctly explains how: hygiene removes pathogens (handwashing kills/removes germs), prevents their entry (clean skin has fewer entry points), and eliminates growth conditions (clean, dry environments discourage microbial growth) — all breaking the chain of infection.
elimination_hints: Both statements are well-established public health facts. The Reason explains the precise mechanism (pathogen removal, blocked entry, hostile growth conditions) that makes hygiene effective.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Arrange the following stages in the natural history of a communicable disease in the correct chronological order from exposure to the pathogen to either recovery or death.
items: I. Clinical stage — signs and symptoms appear, disease is diagnosable and the person seeks medical treatment
II. Exposure and incubation — the pathogen enters the body and multiplies without visible symptoms
III. Recovery, disability or death — the disease resolves, leaves lasting effects, or proves fatal
IV. Prodromal stage — early non-specific symptoms such as mild fever, fatigue and body ache appear
options: A. II → IV → I → III
B. I → III → II → IV
C. IV → II → III → I
D. III → I → IV → II
explanation: Natural history of disease: Exposure and incubation (II, pathogen enters, no symptoms) → Prodromal stage (IV, early non-specific symptoms) → Clinical stage (I, full symptoms, diagnosis and treatment) → Outcome: recovery, disability or death (III). This follows the standard progression taught in epidemiology.
elimination_hints: Exposure/incubation (II) is always the first stage; outcome (III) is always the last. Anchor with these endpoints.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Arrange the following steps of proper handwashing technique as recommended by the WHO in the correct order from start to finish.
items: I. Rinse hands thoroughly under clean running water to remove soap and loosened germs
II. Wet hands with clean running water and apply soap
III. Dry hands using a clean towel or air dry them completely
IV. Rub hands together vigorously for at least twenty seconds, lathering all surfaces including between fingers and under nails
options: A. II → IV → I → III
B. IV → I → III → II
C. I → II → III → IV
D. III → II → IV → I
explanation: WHO handwashing technique: Wet hands and apply soap (II) → Rub vigorously for 20+ seconds covering all surfaces (IV) → Rinse under clean running water (I) → Dry with clean towel or air dry (III). Wet-lather-rinse-dry is the standard sequence.
elimination_hints: Wetting hands and applying soap (II) is always the first step; drying (III) is always the last. Anchor with these.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: True or False: An epidemic refers to the sudden increase in the number of cases of a disease above what is normally expected in a specific geographical area within a short period of time.
options: A. True
B. False
explanation: This statement is True. An epidemic is defined as the occurrence of disease cases in a community or region clearly in excess of normal expectation, concentrated in a specific geographical area and time period. It represents a sudden spike above the baseline level of the disease.
elimination_hints: This is the standard epidemiological definition of an epidemic — sudden increase, above normal expectation, specific area, short time period.
subject: Physical Education

question_text: Study the diagram below that shows the three levels of disease prevention (primary, secondary, tertiary) with examples of health interventions at each level. Based on the diagram, at which prevention level does Health Education have the GREATEST potential impact on reducing disease burden in a population?
options: A. Tertiary prevention — because treating existing patients is always more impactful than preventing new cases

B. Primary prevention — because preventing disease before it occurs through vaccination, hygiene education, nutrition awareness and lifestyle modification reaches the entire population, avoids suffering entirely, and is the most cost-effective approach to reducing overall disease burden
C. Secondary prevention — because screening programmes are the only useful health intervention that exists
D. None of the levels — Health Education has no impact on disease prevention at any level
explanation: Health Education has the greatest impact at the primary prevention level because: it reaches the entire population (not just those already diseased), prevents disease from ever occurring (avoiding suffering, disability and death), is the most cost-effective approach, and creates lasting behaviour changes. While Health Education contributes to all three levels, its population-wide preventive reach at the primary level offers the greatest potential for reducing overall disease burden.
elimination_hints: A prioritises treatment over prevention (less efficient); C claims screening is the only intervention; D denies Health Education's impact. B correctly identifies primary prevention as the most impactful level.
subject: Physical Education
